title: Post-call: NorthView evaluation — cloud APIs vs Redwood (embeddings + rerank tests)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena@northview.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-18T14:05:00-07:00
Subject: Post-call: NorthView evaluation — cloud APIs vs Redwood (embeddings + rerank tests)

Thanks again for the call earlier, Elena — quick recap and proposed next steps so we can move to a short, measurable eval.

Recap (what you emphasized)
- Primary goal: improve legal-doc search recall while lowering per-query cost (especially for high-concurrency retrieval).
- Key metrics you care about: recall@1/5/10, end-to-end p95 latency, and cost per 1M queries.
- Current infra: in-house vector index on GCS + on-prem FAISS for heavy queries; considering cloud-managed vector endpoints from hyperscalers.

What we propose (short matrix you can approve quickly)
1) Retrieval-only baseline (no re-rank): your embeddings + HNSW/IVF variants — measure recall@k and raw vector latency.
2) Embedding dims test: 256 / 512 / 1024 to see recall vs tokenization/size tradeoffs.
3) Rerank pipeline: retrieve topK=100 then rerank (cross-encoder) to top10 — measure added latency and recall delta.
4) End-to-end: measure client-to-result p50/p95 tail latencies for both Redwood-hosted endpoint and your current cloud provider endpoint (we'll use identical input batch).

Concrete asks from NorthView for step 1 (what we need to run):
- A sample of ~300 representative queries + expected ideal doc ids (can be partial/first-pass). Attach sample-queries.csv for format.
- A 10k-document subset (exportable) or a small example index so we can run internal microbenchmarks locally.
- Any hard cost constraint: e.g., target <$X per 1M queries (helps us pick quantization / batch).

What Redwood will deliver (48–72h micro-PoC):
- A short benchmark report with recall@1/5/10, raw retrieval latency, rerank delta, and estimated cost per 1M queries for both Redwood and one hyperscaler API (we'll target the one you mentioned on the call).
- Configuration matrix (embedding dim, index type, batch size, quantization suggestions) and recommended rollout for production.

Attachments: northview-embed-rerank-plan.pdf (benchmark plan) and sample-queries.csv (CSV with query,id).

Next steps / timeline
- If you can share the sample CSV + index subset by end of week we can deliver the short report early next week.
- If helpful, we can schedule a 30m slot for Jordan to walk through the benchmarking plan and answer infra questions.

Thanks — happy to iterate on the test matrix if you want a different emphasis.

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com
(attachment: northview-embed-rerank-plan.pdf; sample-queries.csv)

From: Elena Ruiz <elena@northview.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Bose <samir@acme-search.com>
Date: 2026-07-18T17:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: NorthView evaluation — cloud APIs vs Redwood (embeddings + rerank tests)

Ben — thanks for the clear summary. Quick clarifications before we hand over data: 

- The 10k subset: we can export a 10k subset of our corpus (redacted) by EOD Friday. Good.
- Sample queries: we'll provide ~300 queries; Samir will help format the expected-id column.
- Cost target: we don't have a hard cap yet, but as a rough bar please assume we want under $200 per 1M queries (at current throughput). If Redwood can show a path to 50% savings at similar recall, that's compelling.

One technical question: for rerank, when you say "cross-encoder" are you proposing a hosted model on Redwood or sending to a hyperscaler endpoint? We want both numbers: your hosted rerank (Redwood-managed) and the baseline cloud-managed rerank (to compare latency/cost).

I'll send the CSV and the subset by Friday and add Samir to any follow-ups. Thanks — happy to schedule the 30m with Jordan.

—Elena
Head of Search, NorthView Analytics

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Elena Ruiz <elena@northview.ai>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>, Samir Bose <samir@acme-search.com>
Date: 2026-07-18T18:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-call: NorthView evaluation — cloud APIs vs Redwood (embeddings + rerank tests)

Elena — perfect, thanks. Short answers and additions: 

- Cross-encoder rerank: we'll run two rerank configurations in the micro-PoC: (A) Redwood-hosted cross-encoder (quantized where appropriate) and (B) a callout to the cloud provider endpoint you currently use, with identical inputs, to measure delta.

- Test matrix (what we'll run automatically):
  * Embedding dims: 256 / 512 / 1024
  * Index types: HNSW (default params), IVF+PQ (to show cost/size reductions)
  * Retrieval topK: 50 / 100 / 250 (we'll report recall@1/5/10 for each)
  * Rerank: cross-encoder on top100 -> top10; measure addtl latency.
  * Batch sizes for throughput tests: 1, 8, 32; measure p50/p95/p99 token/response latency.

- Key metrics we'll produce: recall@1/5/10, end-to-end p50/p95, per-query cost (estimate) for 1M queries, GPU/CPU utilization during tests.

- On cost targets: we'll optimize for the <$200 per 1M goal; if we can get ~40–60% savings using batching + quantized embeddings on Redwood while matching recall, we'll highlight that.

I attached the short benchmark plan (northview-embed-rerank-plan.pdf) that maps test names to scripts. Once you drop the CSV + index subset I'll schedule the run and share a dedicated Drive folder with results and a short report.

Would 11am PT Tuesday for a 30m review with Jordan work?

Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
(attachment: northview-embed-rerank-plan.pdf)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-19T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Post-call: NorthView evaluation — cloud APIs vs Redwood (embeddings + rerank tests)

Internal note — forwarding the thread from Elena. Key asks from NorthView: sample CSV + 10k subset by Friday and a desire to see Redwood-hosted rerank vs their cloud endpoint.

Immediate asks for the infra team (Jordan):
- Can you run a micro-benchmark next week that mirrors the plan? Target: embedding dims 256/512/1024, HNSW vs IVF+PQ, retrieve top100 then rerank to top10. Use a 10k doc subset we control; we'll replace with NorthView data when they share it.
- Outputs I need: recall@1/5/10, end-to-end p95 latency, and a back-of-the-envelope per-1M-query cost estimate for each config.

Monica — please sync with Solutions Eng to confirm we can run an apples-to-apples cloud endpoint comparison (we'll need to reproduce their request pattern and include their current provider in the run). Also flag any SOC/NDAs we need before they share the subset.

Jordan — can you confirm availability for a short run Tues morning (11am PT) and whether you need a small box or can run on Dedicated dev cluster? If Dedicated dev cluster is required, I can request a 48h reservation.

I'll capture results in HubSpot and share the Drive folder once the run starts.

Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

(Forwarded attachments: northview-embed-rerank-plan.pdf; sample-queries.csv)

Hi all — thanks Ben. Lina, quick status from Security: we have the latest validated third-party penetration test performed by Redwood Security Partners dated 2024-12-14. Scope summary:

- Target: Redwood hosted inference API (public endpoints), associated auth flows, control-plane APIs, and edge/fargate ingress.
- Exclusions: Dedicated/Private deployments and customer-managed VPCs (those were scoped separately).
- Techniques: external black-box assessment, authenticated API flows, token/session handling, and some targeted dependency analysis.

Findings & remediation highlights:
- Critical: 1 finding (session fixation on an obsolete internal endpoint) — fully remediated, verified in re-test (Jira INF-2451).
- High: 2 findings (improper token expiry handling; insecure S3 presigner usage in older infra) — remediated and backported (Jira INF-2587).
- Medium/Low: remaining items are tracked; planned fixes in our Q3 sprints (see remediation_summary_ACME.xlsx).

What we'll share now:
- Redwood_PenTest_2024-12-14-v2-redacted.pdf — contains executive summary, attack chains, repros with redactions to remove internal IPs and customer-identifying data.
- remediation_summary_ACME.xlsx — mapping of findings to Jira tickets and current status.

Delivery & controls:
- We'll publish the two files to our secure sharing service with a presigned link that expires in 7 days. Link will be encrypted in transit (HTTPS).
- For full vulnerability details (unredacted repros/stack traces) we require a mutual NDA + named contact list; we can move to that if your team needs it.
- Password for the ZIP will be provided out-of-band (SMS to a verified point of contact) or via your PKI key if you have one on record.

Estimated ETA: I can have the secure link and files ready by EOD today (2026-07-18) and Ben will confirm delivery to your inbox.

Subject: Mid-quarter stakeholder readout — scope shift proposals & dependency owners

Team — kicking off the mid-quarter readout thread. Attached is the short deck for the stakeholder readout (slide notes included) and a spreadsheet that lists the proposed re-scopes + suggested dependency owners. High level asks here: 

1) Confirm which scope reductions we approve today (see slide 3: \"non-critical infra experiments\" & \"incubation UX flow\") by EOD Thu 7/16.
2) Sign up dependency owners in the spreadsheet for any item marked \"needs owner\" — owners should be internal (no external handoffs without a partner contact).
3) Vivek / Marcus: call out any infra or model-serving blockers that would prevent a safe QBR roll-forward.
4) Naomi: please confirm whether leadership wants this as a focused 15-min readout or a 30-min Q&A for execs.

Quick notes in the deck: proposed latency optimization sprint can be delayed one sprint if we reassign two infra FTEs to the routing work. The risk is higher per-token cost in short term; I called it out on slide 5.

Thanks — Aisha

title: SOC 2 Type II: cloud provider carve-out & subprocessor mapping — clarification
preview:
From: Sasha Ivanov <sasha.ivanov@edgehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>
Cc: Marco Ruiz <m.ruiz@edgehealth.com>, EdgeHealth Security Team <security@edgehealth.com>
Date: Wed, 15 Jul 2026 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Clarification needed on SOC 2 coverage + subprocessor list

Hi Ben,

Thanks for taking a look at our security questionnaire. A couple of items came back from our risk team that we need to clarify before we can move forward with procurement:

1) Carve-out coverage: does Redwood rely on your cloud provider's (AWS) SOC reports as a carve-out for infrastructure controls? If so, can you confirm which AWS services are considered within the carve-out for Redwood's SOC 2 Type II (EC2, EBS, S3, RDS, KMS, etc.)?

2) Bridge letter: our auditor asked whether Redwood can provide a bridge letter to cover the gap between your SOC period and our audit window (EdgeHealth audit window: 2025-10-01 to 2026-03-31). Will Redwood provide a bridge letter or an attestation excerpt that references covered subservice controls?

3) Subprocessor registry + evidence: please share the up-to-date subprocessor list and any SOC/ISO reports you rely on for subprocessors (e.g., monitoring, backups, analytics). We may need copies or redacted excerpts.

4) Responsibility mapping: we need a short statement mapping shared responsibilities (e.g., who manages encryption keys, backup restoration, network segmentation). Our auditors want this explicitly called out.

Could you let us know an expected timeline? Ideally we need the answers and supporting docs within 5 business days to hit our vendor approval deadline.

Thanks,
Sasha Ivanov
Senior Information Security Manager
EdgeHealth Inc.
+1 (415) 555-0127

Attachments: EdgeHealth_Security_Questionnaire_v3.xlsx (referenced)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>
To: Sasha Ivanov <sasha.ivanov@edgehealth.com>
Cc: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.inference>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.inference>
Date: Wed, 15 Jul 2026 10:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Clarification needed on SOC 2 coverage + subprocessor list

Sasha —

Appreciate the clear ask. Looping in Mina (security) and Kimberly (legal) to provide precise language and the artifacts. Quick answers up front:

- Yes — Redwood’s SOC 2 Type II relies on certain cloud provider (AWS) services as subservice organizations (carve-out) for infrastructure controls. Mina will enumerate the specific AWS services in-scope.
- We can provide a bridge-letter style attestation for the control areas that Redwood owns; legal needs to review the exact wording for auditor consumption.
- We will produce the current subprocessor registry and redacted SOC/ISO excerpts where available.
- We will produce a short shared-responsibility mapping covering encryption (KMS usage), backups, logging retention, and network boundary.

Tentative timeline: 3 business days to provide the subprocessor registry + SOC excerpts; 5 business days to produce a draft bridge letter / control mapping for your auditors. If you need anything expedited, flag the exact auditor expectation and we’ll prioritize.

Mina / Kimberly — can you confirm the list of AWS services Redwood’s SOC references and add any constraints we should call out (e.g., customer-managed keys vs Redwood-managed keys)? Also attaching the SOC excerpt and a working draft of a bridge letter for visibility.

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.inference
Attachment references: redwood_soc2_typeii-excerpt.pdf, provider-soc-audit-bridgeletter-draft.docx

From: Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.inference>
To: Sasha Ivanov <sasha.ivanov@edgehealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.inference>
Date: Wed, 15 Jul 2026 13:26:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Clarification needed on SOC 2 coverage + subprocessor list

Hi Sasha —

Thanks for the detailed request. I’ve outlined the items below to make this consumable for your auditor. If you want, we can convert this into a one-page PDF mapping.

1) AWS services referenced in Redwood’s SOC 2 Type II (examples by category):
   - Compute/Storage: EC2, EBS, S3 (note: S3 used for transient/asset storage; long-term backups are handled via our backup subprocessor)
   - Database: RDS (managed), DynamoDB for session store (where applicable)
   - Networking: VPC, ELB, Route53 (network boundary services)
   - Identity/Keys: AWS KMS (we support both customer-managed and Redwood-managed CMKs depending on contract)
   - Monitoring/Logging: CloudWatch, CloudTrail (used for infrastructure-level logs)

   These services are treated as subservice organizations in our audit (carve-out for infrastructure controls). Our SOC report explicitly notes reliance on AWS for physical and hypervisor controls.

2) Bridge letter / attestation:
   - Redwood can provide a tailored bridge-letter attestation that: (a) references our SOC 2 test period and scope, (b) describes which AWS services are carved out, and (c) states control ownership for the covered areas.
   - Limitations: Bridge letters cannot extend controls Redwood does not operate (e.g., AWS hypervisor patching); they also cannot assert the internal testing performed by AWS beyond what is documented in AWS’s SOC.

3) Subprocessor registry & evidence:
   - I’ll attach the current registry (subprocessor_registry.csv) listing backup providers, monitoring vendors, analytics providers, CI/CD tools. For subprocessors with attestations, we’ll provide redacted SOC/ISO reports where permitted by the vendor.

4) Shared responsibility mapping (high-level):
   - Redwood: application access controls, API auth, data-in-transit encryption, application-level logging and alerting, vulnerability management for the Redwood service surface, encryption key usage for Redwood-managed keys.
   - Customer (EdgeHealth): responsibility for customer-controlled keys when selecting customer-managed KMS, data classification, tenant-level access policies, and any customer-provided integrations (e.g., on-prem connectors).
   - Cloud provider (AWS): physical security, hypervisor/host platform, some infrastructure network controls, and the underlying managed service security for the services listed above.

Requested follow-ups:
   - Please confirm which Redwood regions/accounts you will run in (us-west-2, eu-west-1, etc.) so I can include region-specific evidence where applicable.
   - Confirm your audit window (you listed 2025-10-01 to 2026-03-31) — we can prepare a bridge-letter that references that period.

Attachments to follow in a separate email: redwood_subservice_mapping.xlsx, subprocessor_registry.csv, redwood_soc2_typeii-excerpt.pdf

Cheers,
Mina Haddad
Head of Security Operations | Redwood Inference
mina.haddad@redwood.inference

title: Next steps and 30-min demo availability
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Rachel Liu <rachel.liu@neptuneanalytics.com>
Cc: sales@neptuneanalytics.com
Date: Thu, 15 Jul 2026 14:05:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: quick recap + 30-min demo availability

Hi Rachel —

Thanks again for taking time today. Quick recap from our discovery: 
- Primary goal: reduce per-query latency for the customer-facing recommendation engine and lower inference cost at 95th percentile.
- Current setup: in-house fine-tuned model on single-region GPUs, limited autoscaling and no prefix caching.
- Key constraints: data residency (US), planned Q4 launch, and SSO required.

Suggested next step: a 30-minute product demo + technical Q&A where our solutions engineer can walk through routing/quantization options and autoscaling patterns. Can you confirm which of these windows works for your team? I included times in PT / ET below: 

Option A — Tue Jul 21, 10:00–10:30 PT / 13:00–13:30 ET
Option B — Wed Jul 22, 15:00–15:30 PT / 18:00–18:30 ET
Option C — Thu Jul 23, 08:00–08:30 PT / 11:00–11:30 ET

Date: Fri, 10 Jul 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Hosted API baseline + competitor pricing (OpenAI/Anthropic/Together/AWS)

Ben —

Thanks for the quick intro last week. We're in the middle of evaluating hosted API options for real-time product features (chat + embeddings + reranking) and need a compact baseline to compare economics across vendors. Specifically, can you send:

- A simple hosted API rate card (per-token gen, per-embedding, inference throughput tiers) for Redwood (baseline, trial pricing if any, and committed discounts)
- Any notes on how you price streaming vs non-streaming calls and KV/prefix caching impacts on billable tokens
- A short competitor snapshot (OpenAI, Anthropic, Together, AWS Marketplace) — rough unit pricing or relative index is fine

Our use case: 10M tokens/month (chat), 500k embeddings/month, 3k QPS peak for short-lived sessions during events. We care about tail latency SLA and predictable committed pricing.

Would prefer a short 30-min call next week after we review the packet. cc'ing my procurement lead if we proceed.

Thanks,
Sylvia
Sylvia Cho
Head of Product, AcmeAI
Sylvia.cho@acmeai.com

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sylvia Cho <sylvia.cho@acmeai.com>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Jul 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Hosted API baseline + competitor pricing (OpenAI/Anthropic/Together/AWS)

Hi Sylvia —

Thanks — I put together a short baseline and a couple of comparators (attached: redwood_ratecard_v2.pdf). High level callouts below so you can triage quickly:

- Redwood hosted baseline: pay-as-you-go + committed tiers. Baseline gen: $0.0009 / prompt token, $0.0018 / completion token for our 7B family; embeddings $0.002 per 1k vectors (quantized).
- Trial / POC: we can offer a 25% one-month trial credit for new accounts running POC traffic with a short-term commitment (typical ask).
- Committed discounts: 10-25% depending on 3–12 month commit and unit volumes (higher for dedicated pools).
- Streaming: billable token rules are the same; streaming reduces perceived latency but doesn't change token accounting. KV/prefix caching: significant reduction on repeat prompts — we provide tooling to estimate hit rates; caching does not alter contract units but lowers cost per effective produced token.

Competitor quick notes (very rough):
- OpenAI: premium on generation; strong for large LLMs, less aggressive embedding pricing in this tier.
- Anthropic: competitive gen pricing for instruction models; variable on throughput.
- Together: often lower on embeddings and research models; can be competitive on committed volumes.
- AWS Marketplace: sometimes higher list prices, but negotiable via marketplace credits for enterprise deals.

I've cc'd Neha from our pricing team to help with formal quotes and to scope contract language if you want to progress. If you can share a 30-min slot next Wed/Thu I will bring a one-pager that maps your 10M tokens / 500k embeddings profile to expected monthly spend at baseline and two committed tiers.

Short summary attachment: redwood_ratecard_v2.pdf

Best,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1-415-555-0198

Attachment: redwood_ratecard_v2.pdf (18KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sylvia Cho <sylvia.cho@acmeai.com>, Devin Park <devin@together.ai>
Cc: Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>, Lauren Bishop <lauren_bishop@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 14 Jul 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Hosted API competitor sweep: pricing baseline & match options

Sylvia —

Appended below is a short consolidated baseline and two proposed commercial paths. I kept the math simple so your procurement team can parallel compare. If you'd like, we can spin this into a formal quote and procurement packet after the brief call.

1) Baseline (Pay-as-you-go, Redwood hosted) — monthly est for AcmeAI profile (10M gen tokens, 500k embeddings):
- Gen prompt tokens: $0.0009 / token => $9,000
- Gen completion tokens: $0.0018 / token => $18,000
- Embeddings: $0.002 / 1k => $1,000
- Estimated platform & egress: $650
- Total baseline (est): $28,650 / month

2) POC / Short-term (1 month) — trial offer
- 25% credit applied to first month for POC traffic (applies to usage up to 150% of forecast).
- POC protected overage: first month capped at 30% above committed estimate.

3) Committed options (volume or reserved capacity):
- 3-month commit ($25k/mo equivalent): 15% discount on unit rates => est $24,352 / mo
- 6-month commit ($50k/mo equivalent): 20% discount => est $22,920 / mo
- 12-month / reserved Dedicated option: 25%+ discount and SLA guarantees (exact pricing tied to pool size).

Competitor quick index (directional):
- OpenAI: often +10–30% vs Redwood baseline on completion-heavy workloads (higher on large LLMs).
- Together: competitive on embeddings, in some cases ~15–20% cheaper; gen varies by model and may require committed volumes to match Redwood's latency guarantees.
- Anthropic: close on instruction tuning; pricing depends on model family.
- AWS Marketplace: list prices can be higher; marketplace credits sometimes change net economics.

Attachments: redwood_ratecard_v2.pdf (baseline), together_pricing_snapshot.xlsx, openai_rfp_response.pdf.

From: Lena Park <lena@alderbio.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: GPU lead times + MAP template request

Hi Chloe,

Thanks again for the product walkthrough yesterday — the demo was helpful. We want to build a mutual action plan so our security and procurement teams can align on evaluation milestones and the capacity commitment window. Two immediate asks:

1) What are typical lead times for Dedicated vs Private (on-prem) deployments once we sign a commitment?
2) Can you drop a MAP template or a draft timeline we can place in our internal review?

We expect the evaluation to include a 2-week performance POC, a security questionnaire pass (SOC2 evidence), and an acceptance signoff before any capacity commit. Our finance team is sensitive to tranche-based commitments — they'd like a phased delivery plan.

Looping in our procurement lead in the next note.

Best,
Lena Park
Head of Platform Engineering
Alder Bio

[Attachment: procurement_schedule.pdf (pdf, 48KB)]

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Lena Park <lena@alderbio.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Jul 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: GPU lead times + MAP template request

Hi Lena — thanks for the clear asks. Attached is a working MAP (Alder_MAP_v3.xlsx) that maps milestones, owners, and gating criteria. Key points up front so we have alignment:

- Lead times:
  - Redwood Dedicated (reserved pool): typical procurement-to-availability window is 8–12 weeks after signed commitment + PO. This covers GPU allocation, capacity validation, and network/VPC setup.
  - Redwood Private (customer VPC / on-prem assisted install): 12–16 weeks typical due to data center scheduling, rack/cabling and customer-site validation.

- Recommended tranche approach (we've used this successfully with similar biotech customers):
  1) Pilot tranche (phase 1) — 4 GPUs (A100 80GB equiv) for the 2-week POC and initial eval. Delivery target: within 2–4 weeks after commitment if we leverage existing Dedicated pools; on-prem will be longer.
  2) Ramp tranche (phase 2) — scale to 12 GPUs (mix of A100/H100) for extended load testing and early production. Target: +6–8 weeks.
  3) Production tranche (phase 3) — target 20–24 GPUs for sustained throughput; final sizing adjusts after quality and throughput evals.

- MAP timeline (high level):
  - Week 0: Sign MAP + PO intent (DocuSign)
  - Weeks 1–2: Performance POC (benchmarks, latency SLOs, token throughput)
  - Weeks 3–4: Security/Compliance review (SOC2 artifacts, retention policy, KMS)
  - Weeks 4–6: Acceptance and production handoff for phase 2

I've included estimated cost deltas for tranche transitions in the attached workbook. For Alder's stated target (multimodal inference at 2–3 reqs/sec sustained, 2k token average), our rough starting recommendation is 4 GPUs for POC => 12 GPUs to hit a 3x headroom for early production. Ben can add more specifics on throughput and model choices below.

Next steps: if you can confirm procurement point-of-contact and target close date, we'll produce a final DocuSign package and an SOW appendix with tranche milestones.

Thanks —
Chloe Martin
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference

[Attachment: Alder_MAP_v3.xlsx (xlsx, 92KB)]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>, Lena Park <lena@alderbio.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Fri, 03 Jul 2026 14:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: GPU lead times + MAP template request (technical sizing)

Hi team — quick technical addendum to Chloe's note with concrete sizing assumptions so Alder can validate against internal perf targets:

- Baseline workload assumption (from discovery): 2.5 requests/sec sustained, 2k-token average, model family LlamaX-70B (float16) / quantized variant.
- Pilot (4x A100 80GB equiv): expected aggregate throughput ~10–12 r/s depending on batching and KV-cache hit rates. Latency SLOs (p50/p95) likely 200ms/600ms for 2k tokens with continuous batching.
- Ramp (12 GPUs): throughput target ~30–36 r/s with burst headroom; supports simultaneous eval suites and early production traffic.
- Production (20 GPUs): design for 50–60 r/s sustained with 30–40% headroom for peak windows.

Operational considerations:
- Enable prefix KV caching for evaluation to reduce tokens-per-request on repeated prompts.
- Use mixed quantization (nf4/4-bit for non-categorical models) to reduce memory footprint if model quality allows.
- We recommend a staging inference endpoint that mirrors production for acceptance testing; this avoids noisy neighbor issues when on shared Dedicated pools.

Attached SOC2 evidence excerpt for Alder's security reviewers (this is the subset they asked for). For on-prem installs, expect additional 2–4 week validation for rack/network and HSM/KMS integration.

—Ben
Ben Carter
Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference

Hi Lena — thanks for the clear asks. Attached is a working MAP (Alder_MAP_v3.xlsx) that maps milestones, owners, and gating criteria. Key points up front so we have alignment:

- Lead times:
  - Redwood Dedicated (reserved pool): typical procurement-to-availability window is 8–12 weeks after signed commitment + PO. This covers GPU allocation, capacity validation, and network/VPC setup.
  - Redwood Private (customer VPC / on-prem assisted install): 12–16 weeks typical due to data center scheduling, rack/cabling and customer-site validation.

- Recommended tranche approach (we've used this successfully with similar biotech customers):
  1) Pilot tranche (phase 1) — 4 GPUs (A100 80GB equiv) for the 2-week POC and initial eval. Delivery target: within 2–4 weeks after commitment if we leverage existing Dedicated pools; on-prem will be longer.
  2) Ramp tranche (phase 2) — scale to 12 GPUs (mix of A100/H100) for extended load testing and early production. Target: +6–8 weeks.
  3) Production tranche (phase 3) — target 20–24 GPUs for sustained throughput; final sizing adjusts after quality and throughput evals.

- MAP timeline (high level):
  - Week 0: Sign MAP + PO intent (DocuSign)
  - Weeks 1–2: Performance POC (benchmarks, latency SLOs, token throughput)
  - Weeks 3–4: Security/Compliance review (SOC2 artifacts, retention policy, KMS)
  - Weeks 4–6: Acceptance and production handoff for phase 2

I've included estimated cost deltas for tranche transitions in the attached workbook. For Alder's stated target (multimodal inference at 2–3 reqs/sec sustained, 2k token average), our rough starting recommendation is 4 GPUs for POC => 12 GPUs to hit a 3x headroom for early production. title: MedCore inference readout + Redwood perf pack
preview:
From: Chloe Martin <chloe@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Patel <aisha.patel@medcore-health.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-12T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Post-demo readout + quick artifacts

Hi Aisha —

Thanks again for the time on today's demo. Quick recap from our side and the artifacts we promised:

1) High-level recommendation (based on the prompts you shared): two candidate model families — a 13B quantized variant for cost-sensitive real-time assists, and a 33B FP16 variant for higher-fidelity clinical summarization when latency budget is larger.
2) Performance samples: TPS and p50/p95 latency snapshots below (short summary) + links to full traces.
3) Prompt pack: curated prompt set adapted from your sample transcripts for regression checks.

Attachments included: redwood-verified-perf-pack-medcore.pdf (high-level perf profiles), prompt-set-medcore-v1.txt, inference-traces-medcore-0706.json (sanitized traces).

Action items / next steps I recommend:
- Your engineering/CTO: confirm pilot window (2–4 weeks) and preferred SLAs.
- We'll provision a sandbox with the 13B quantized model and the 33B FP16 model for side-by-side evaluation.
- Jordan will share the Redwood-verified performance pack and a short checklist for regression guardrails.

If you want, we can set a short 30-minute alignment call tomorrow to lock the pilot scope.

Best,
Chloe Martin
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
chloe@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0194

---



From: Aisha Patel <aisha.patel@medcore-health.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe@redwood.com>
Cc: Tom Reynolds <tom.reynolds@medcore-health.com>
Date: 2026-07-12T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo readout + quick artifacts

Thanks Chloe — this is helpful. Quick clarifying Qs from our side: 

- For the 13B quantized model, what p95 latencies should we expect at 8 TPS (text gen, avg prompt length ~400 tokens)?
- Any observed quality regressions vs the 33B when using your prompt pack? We want to avoid hallucination on medication fields.
- Pilot logistics: Can you confirm whether logs (for audit) include per-token timing and request traces? We need that for our security/compliance review.

Tom and I are available for a call tomorrow afternoon if that helps.

Appreciate it — Aisha
Aisha Patel
Head of AI Integrations, MedCore Health
aisha.patel@medcore-health.com | +1 (617) 555-0129

---



From: Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Patel <aisha.patel@medcore-health.com>, Tom Reynolds <tom.reynolds@medcore-health.com>
Cc: Chloe Martin <chloe@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-12T13:18:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo readout + quick artifacts

Hi Aisha —

Quick answers and pointers inline:

- 13B quantized @ 8 TPS (avg payload ~400 tokens): expect p50 ~85ms/token, p95 ~220ms/token on our Dedicated NVLink pool. End-to-end generation p95 for a 400-token completion is ~880ms. Those numbers map to ~8–10 RPS equivalent when pipelined; we include the full traces in inference-traces-medcore-0706.json.

- Quality regressions: In our internal sweep using your prompt pack the 13B Q8 profile produced similar entity recall for medication names (~95% precision on the curated set) but had slightly more partial matches on multi-drug instructions. The 33B FP16 variant consistently reduced partial-match rate by ~6 percentage points. That tradeoff is typical: stronger prefix handling/long-range dependencies on the larger model.

- Audit/logging: yes — sandbox provides per-request traces (timestamped), per-token timing, and a sanitized KV cache snapshot option for debugging. We can also stream logs into your Splunk/CloudWatch destination with IAM role.

Attachments: I attached the small "redwood-verified-perf-pack-medcore.pdf" (single-page executive summary) and a short checklist for regression tests (see attached). Full perf URL: https://app.redwood.ai/perf/medcore-MC-8891

If helpful, here's a suggested mini-pilot plan:
- Week 0: Provision sandbox + ingest sample prompt set (you provide 10k representative exchanges).
- Week 1–2: Run parallel evaluation across the two model variants; collect quality metrics (entity recall, hallucination rate), and perf traces at target TPS.
- Week 3: Review results; decide whether to proceed with Dedicated capacity or tune quantization profile.

I can jump on the call tomorrow at 2pm PT. Chloe — can you confirm?

Thanks,
Jordan Blake
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
jordan@redwood.com | +1 (646) 555-0233

---



From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Patel <aisha.patel@medcore-health.com>, Tom Reynolds <tom.reynolds@medcore-health.com>
Cc: Chloe Martin <chloe@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-13T10:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo readout + quick artifacts

Hi team —

Just adding capacity/ops notes from our side:

- If you want deterministic p95 under the 1s target for 400-token outputs, we recommend the 33B FP16 variant on Dedicated with the following config: 2x A100-80GBs in a colocated pool, batch size capped at 4, and prefix caching enabled. That configuration gives consistent p95 headroom at 8–12 TPS.

- Cost estimate (very rough): 33B FP16 Dedicated pilot (2 weeks) — estimated incremental infra cost ~$6,400; 13B Q8 sandbox run (2 weeks) — ~$1,100. We can provide a formal pilot quote if you want to proceed.

- Compliance note: our sandbox supports redaction hooks before logs leave the VPC and has HMAC-signed audit tokens for each trace.

Next steps we need from MedCore:
1) Confirm sample payloads (10k rows) and desired pilot start date window.
2) Decide whether to run both model variants in parallel or start with 13B Q8 and expand if quality delta warrants.

Happy to include our finance/SRE for the formal quote and onboarding if you confirm the pilot.

Thanks — Marcus
Marcus Lin
Director, Dedicated Products | Redwood Inference
marcus@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0456

---



From: Tom Reynolds <tom.reynolds@medcore-health.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe@redwood.com>
Cc: Aisha Patel <aisha.patel@medcore-health.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-14T15:46:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Post-demo readout + quick artifacts

Thanks everyone — this is great context. We reviewed the perf pack and the short checklist. Aisha and I discussed and our preference is:

- Run the parallel pilot (both 13B Q8 and 33B FP16) so we can capture the quality delta under realistic load.
- Target pilot start: week of July 27 (gives our infra team two weeks to prepare sample payloads and security signoffs).
- Please send a formal pilot quote (Marcus) and a short onboarding doc for our infra (Jordan). We will also raise a HubSpot ticket for contract/procurement next week.

Appreciate the clear next steps — Tom
Tom Reynolds
VP Product & Platform, MedCore Health
tom.reynolds@medcore-health.com | +1 (617) 555-0177

title: POC scope & access checklist — follow-up from discovery (next steps)
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>
To: Eleanor Shaw <eleanor.shaw@orchidhealth.com>, Jason Liu <j.liu@orchidhealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.inference>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.inference>
Date: Sun, 12 Jul 2026 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: POC scope & access checklist — follow-up from discovery (next steps)

Thanks again for a productive call today — appreciate the time, Eleanor and Jason. Quick recap + proposed next steps so we can get a pilot moving next week.

Top pain points you called out:
- Clinical note summarization has inconsistent structure across providers and causes downstream NPS drops.
- Search/ranking for patient-facing content returns low-relevance results for 25–30% of queries.
- Latency spikes during peak hours are impacting UX.

Primary goals for a 4-week pilot:
- Demonstrate structured summarization with consistent JSON outputs for 80% of sample notes.
- Improve reranking precision (top-3 relevance) by measurable delta against current baseline.
- Meet latency target: p95 under 200ms for the study workload.

Proposed pilot scope (starter):
- Two endpoints: 1) Summarization (structured JSON), 2) Reranking for search results.
- Evaluation set: 50 prompt/examples for each endpoint (we'll co-develop).
- Success metrics: precision@3 uplift, structured output consistency (sample-based review), p95 latency.

Immediate asks from Orchid to unblock kickoff:
1) Sample dataset: 50 representative notes and a short CSV of queries + expected ranking (anonymized).
2) SSO / admin contact for SAML/SCIM provisioning (needed to create test accounts).
3) Network: allowlist Redwood staging IPs (I list them below) or add a temporary egress rule for the service account.
4) IAM: create a read-only S3 bucket or service account with read access for the pilot artifacts.

Redwood staging IPs (allowlist):
- 34.210.12.45
- 34.210.12.46
- 34.210.12.47

Attachments: POC-scope-v1.xlsx (high-level scope & timeline) attached.

If that looks good I'll draft a kickoff plan (owners, timeline, prompt set schedule) and send a short checklist for your infra team to follow. Proposed kickoff: Mon Jul 19 — 30 min technical alignment + access handoff.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.inference | +1 (415) 555-0182

Redwood Inference
Attachment: POC-scope-v1.xlsx (excel, 32KB)

Date: Mon, 13 Jul 2026 13:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC scope & access checklist — follow-up from discovery (next steps)

Thanks — great, appreciate the timeline clarity. Below is a concise kickoff plan + checklist so your infra and product teams know owners and timing.

POC Kickoff plan (week 0 — prep)
- Owner (Redwood): Monica Patel (Solutions Engineering) — technical lead for integration, model selection, eval harness.
- Owner (Redwood): Avery Johnson (Sales Engineering) — orchestration, routing policy recommendations.
- Owner (Orchid): Samir Patel (Infrastructure) — SSO, service account, firewall rules.
- AE: Ben Carter — program owner, weekly status.

Week 1 (Jul 19–23) — Kickoff & access
- Mon Jul 19 10:00 PT: 30-min technical kickoff (access handoff, test dataset sanity check).
- Provide: read-only S3 path or service account + temporary credentials, allowlist Redwood staging IPs.
- Deliverable: Redwood validates access, ingests sample set, shares initial prompt set (50 examples) by Thu Jul 22.

Week 2 (Jul 26–30) — Eval & iterations
- Redwood runs model variants against the prompt set; we collect structured outputs and ranking results.
- Daily syncs optional (15 min) with Monica & Samir as needed.

Week 3 (Aug 2–6) — Optimization
- Apply batching/caching and comparator reranking adjustments; measure p95 latency and precision@3.

Week 4 (Aug 9–15) — Presentation & wrap
- Deliver final report covering metrics, example outputs, operational notes, and recommended rollout options.

Success criteria (short list):
- Structured summarization: >= 80% consistency on the 50-sample evaluation (manual spot check).
- Reranking: measurable precision@3 uplift vs baseline (we'll compare to your CSV labels).
- Performance: p95 latency < 200ms on staging workload.

Access & infra checklist for Samir (quick)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.inference>
To: Eleanor Shaw <eleanor.shaw@orchidhealth.com>, Jason Liu <j.liu@orchidhealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.inference>, Avery Johnson <avery.johnson@redwood.inference>
Date: Sun, 12 Jul 2026 15:03:00 -0700
Subject: POC scope & access checklist — follow-up from discovery (next steps)

Thanks again for a productive call today — appreciate the time, Eleanor and Jason. Quick recap + proposed next steps so we can get a pilot moving next week.

Top pain points you called out:
- Clinical note summarization has inconsistent structure across providers and causes downstream NPS drops.
- Search/ranking for patient-facing content returns low-relevance results for 25–30% of queries.
- Latency spikes during peak hours are impacting UX.

Primary goals for a 4-week pilot:
- Demonstrate structured summarization with consistent JSON outputs for 80% of sample notes.
- Improve reranking precision (top-3 relevance) by measurable delta against current baseline.
- Meet latency target: p95 under 200ms for the study workload.

Proposed pilot scope (starter):
- Two endpoints: 1) Summarization (structured JSON), 2) Reranking for search results.
- Evaluation set: 50 prompt/examples for each endpoint (we'll co-develop).
- Success metrics: precision@3 uplift, structured output consistency (sample-based review), p95 latency.

Immediate asks from Orchid to unblock kickoff:
1) Sample dataset: 50 representative notes and a short CSV of queries + expected ranking (anonymized).
2) SSO / admin contact for SAML/SCIM provisioning (needed to create test accounts).
3) Network: allowlist Redwood staging IPs (I list them below) or add a temporary egress rule for the service account.
4) IAM: create a read-only S3 bucket or service account with read access for the pilot artifacts.

Redwood staging IPs (allowlist):
- 34.210.12.45
- 34.210.12.46
- 34.210.12.47

Attachments: POC-scope-v1.xlsx (high-level scope & timeline) attached.

If that looks good I'll draft a kickoff plan (owners, timeline, prompt set schedule) and send a short checklist for your infra team to follow. Proposed kickoff: Mon Jul 19 — 30 min technical alignment + access handoff.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.inference | +1 (415) 555-0182

Redwood Inference

- Owner (Redwood): Avery Johnson (Sales Engineering) — orchestration, routing policy recommendations.

Week 1 (Jul 19–23) — Kickoff & access

Week 2 (Jul 26–30) — Eval & iterations

- Apply batching/caching and comparator reranking adjustments; measure p95 latency and precision@3.

title: Novus pilot: tempo uplift & throttle handoff — coordination needed
preview:
From: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>
To: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
Cc: Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-13T08:14:00Z
Subject: Novus pilot: tempo uplift & throttle handoff — coordination needed

Team —

Novus is going live with their closed beta on Monday (they confirmed nights-of-traffic starting 2026-07-18). They just sent a last-minute ramp schedule and are asking for a temporary ingress uplift during the first 72 hours of the pilot plus a short-term higher concurrency window for warmstarts. Key asks from Novus:
- 2x nominal RPS for first 48 hours (expected peak: 220 RPS, baseline 110)
- Temporary increase to token throughput for streaming requests (peak ~1.6M tokens/min)
- Warmstart KV cache retention for conversation sessions (they expect 10–15s between follow-up calls)

I need us to confirm feasibility and the operational checklist: entitlements from Billing, IP whitelist, route tag + canary rollout, and an emergency throttling rollback plan. Priya — what product toggles do we need to enable for warmstart retention and fallback model variant? Karthik — can infra support a 2x uplift for 48 hours with a safe drain plan? Marcus/Evelyn — any telemetry/alerting we should pre-configure in Console?

I've attached their forecast spreadsheet and the entitlement request they emailed. I'll loop in Novus after we agree on the internal playbook.

Thanks,
Avery
Avery Johnson
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

Attachments: novus_warmstart_forecast.xlsx (spreadsheet), entitlement_request_novus.pdf (PDF)


From: Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-13T09:02:00Z
Subject: Re: Novus pilot: tempo uplift & throttle handoff — coordination needed

Thanks for the heads-up, Avery. I skimmed the forecast — helpful. A few product/roadmap clarifications before we commit to an entitlement:

1) Warmstart KV semantics: we can increase per-session KV retention to 60s for the pilot but it will impact memory usage on the serving pool. We should scope which routes actually need this — recommend enabling for the /chat/stream route only.

2) Fallback model variant: we can prepare a quantized 8-bit variant (same architecture) that reduces GPU token cost ~40%. We need ~24 hours to validate quality on their smoke prompts and to add the variant to the routing policy.

3) Throttle window & quotas: product can support a timebound ephemeral entitlement (48 hours max) that maps to a temp rate limit and a post-window automatic rollback. We should specify exact start/end UTC times in the entitlement doc.

Questions:
- Expected percent of requests that are streaming vs non-streaming? The forecast lists tokens but not streaming mix.
- Do they plan long-running streaming sessions (>30s)? That changes KV and connection resource planning.

If we get answers by EOD we can ship the fallback model and the entitlement change in time.

—Priya
Priya Natarajan
Product Manager, Inference

On 2026-07-13 08:14, Avery Johnson wrote:
> Team —
> 
> Novus is going live with their closed beta on Monday (they confirmed nights-of-traffic starting 2026-07-18). They just sent a last-minute ramp schedule and are asking for a temporary ingress uplift during the first 72 hours of the pilot plus a short-term higher concurrency window for warmstarts.


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik_iyer@redwood.com>
To: Avery Johnson <avery_johnson@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya_natarajan@redwood.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>, Sean Gallagher <sean_gallagher@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-07-13T10:45:00Z
Subject: Re: Novus pilot: tempo uplift & throttle handoff — coordination needed

We can support a temporary 2x uplift for 48 hours but a few infra caveats: 

- Pool capacity: current dedicated pool for pilot-grade tenants has ~30% spare headroom; pushing to 2x will require borrowing from the shared burst pool. That is feasible but we need an explicit borrow-and-return schedule to avoid impacting other pilots.

- KV cache retention: increasing retention to 60s is ok for the /chat/stream route only. We'll need to set per-route cache limits (max entries per session) — recommend max 256 tokens stored per session as a guardrail.

- Canary & rollback: We'll publish a route tag (novus-pilot-48h) and do a canary rollout at 10% for the first hour. If error rate or 95p latency increases beyond 2x baseline we'll auto-roll back. We need Product to sign off on the rollback SLA thresholds.

- Whitelisting: Novus must provide egress IP ranges for their client traffic (or we can accept a JWT assertion). Marcus — please coordinate with Network for the 2–3 CIDR blocks.

I'll push a runbook for ops and a short playbook for on-call to the shared drive once we confirm times.

-Karthik
Karthik Iyer
Solutions Engineering / Infra

On 2026-07-13 09:02, Priya Natarajan wrote:
> Thanks for the heads-up, Avery. I skimmed the forecast — helpful. A few product/roadmap clarifications before we commit to an entitlement...

Subject: Re: Novus pilot: tempo uplift & throttle handoff — coordination needed

Thanks for coordinating. Quick answers from our side: 
- Streaming vs non-streaming: ~70% streaming (short bursts), 30% normal request/response. Median session ~12s, 95p ~45s.
- Ramp schedule: 2026-07-18 02:00–08:00 UTC (nights), 2026-07-19 scaled ramp 00:00–10:00 UTC. We'll provide a CSV with our expected IP egress ranges by EOD today.
- Procurement will review the entitlement doc; they asked whether we can get a one-time credit if we exceed the uptick during the pilot window (they want a safety net).

We appreciate the fallback variant offer — would like the 8-bit option on standby.

Best,
Alice Park

title: Clarify renewal notice & convenience termination concessions — Polaris Health MSA
preview:
From: Elaine Wang <elaine.wang@polarishealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@polarishealth.com
Date: Sat, 11 Jul 2026 09:14:00 -07:00
Subject: Polaris MSA — auto-renew notice window & termination for convenience

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the demo last week. Procurement at Polaris reviewed the MSA and we have two outstanding items before we can route to Legal:

1) Auto-renew: Your standard MSA currently has a 90-day auto-renew notice window. For our internal approval cycles we typically need a 60-day opt-out window (for both pilot and enterprise contracts). Is Redwood open to a 60-day notice?

2) Termination for convenience: We prefer a mutual termination for convenience but need clarity on any termination fee or transition support. Our security/compliance team asked whether Redwood would agree to a 30-day pro-rata wind-down and a 30-day notice or whether there is a termination fee.

Attached are our procurement requirements and timeline. We are targeting a signed order form by 7/23 — ideally earlier. Can you confirm whether the 60-day notice and the proposed 30-day wind-down are acceptable, or provide alternative language Redwood prefers?

Appreciate a quick call if easier.

Thanks,
Elaine Wang
Senior Procurement Manager
Polaris Health
[Attachment: Polaris_Procurement_Requirements.pdf (pdf)]

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Elaine Wang <elaine.wang@polarishealth.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Jul 2026 11:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Polaris MSA — auto-renew notice window & termination for convenience

Hi Elaine — great to hear from you and thanks for the clear asks. Looping in Karthik (Legal) and Laura (Sales Ops) to confirm. Quick response below and we can follow up with tracked redlines this week.

Summary of Redwood's initial position (open to compromise):
- Auto-renew: We prefer 90-day notice for enterprise MSA to ensure capacity planning and orderly rollback. That said, we can accept 60 days for Polaris given pilot->enterprise path — would ask for a one-year minimum initial term and a 60-day opt-out for renewals thereafter.
- Termination for convenience: Redwood generally allows mutual termination for convenience with a pro-rated refund for prepaid fees and a customer-obligated transition fee for active dedicated capacity (if applicable). For standard hosted customers we usually require either: (a) 30 days' fees as a termination fee, or (b) 30 days' notice with Redwood providing 30 days of transition support (whichever is greater).

Proposed compromise we can put in redline form: 60-day written notice to opt-out of renewal; mutual termination for convenience permitted with either 30 days' transition support or payment equal to 30 days of average monthly fees (customer's choice). This keeps your 60-day preference while protecting Redwood operationally.

I'll attach a draft with tracked changes (Redwood_MSA_v3_redlines.docx). Karthik/Laura — can you confirm legal + billing are comfortable with the language below; if so I'll send formal redlines to Elaine and Polaris Legal.

Proposed snippet (informal):
\"Either party may terminate this Agreement for convenience upon sixty (60) days' prior written notice. Following receipt of such notice Redwood will provide up to thirty (30) days of transition support, or Customer may elect to pay a termination fee equal to thirty (30) days of the then-applicable monthly charges, pro-rated as applicable.\"

Thanks — happy to hop on a 20–30 minute call if Polaris would like to walk through points.

Best,
Ava Chen
Head of Customer Success, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: Redwood_MSA_v3_redlines.docx (docx)]

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 11 Jul 2026 13:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Polaris MSA — auto-renew notice window & termination for convenience

Ava / Laura — quick legal view. Overall the compromise Ava proposed is reasonable for the typical enterprise pilot -> paid conversion we see. A couple of notes for drafting:

- Auto-renew language: make sure the clause states \"automatic renewal for successive one (1) year terms\" and clearly sets the notice window for non-renewal to \"not less than sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current term.\" That prevents ambiguity around partial-month timing.

- Termination for convenience: Redwood should keep the customer choice (transition support v. termination fee) but we should also add a short carve-out for dedicated capacity that requires longer notice (e.g., 90 days) or reimbursement of decommitment costs. Suggest adding: \"Notwithstanding the foregoing, termination of Agreement with respect to Dedicated Capacity requires ninety (90) days' prior written notice and reimbursement of decommissioning or re-provisioning costs reasonably incurred by Redwood.\"

- Data/Export obligations: add explicit export window of 30 days following termination and include obligations for customer to request data export in writing during that window. (Polaris's security team will expect this explicitly called out.)

If you want, I can prepare tracked redlines to the MSA and a one-page summary for your procurement tracker. Also recommend we set an internal approval threshold: if Polaris requests waiver of the dedicated-capacity carve-out, escalate to Legal Director.

Karthik
Associate General Counsel, Redwood Inference

On internal coordination: Laura, can you confirm whether Polaris's Order Form shows any dedicated pool line items (I saw their procurement doc but want to double-check billing lines)? If not, the 60-day path is straightforward.


From: Elaine Wang <elaine.wang@polarishealth.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@polarishealth.com
Date: Mon, 13 Jul 2026 15:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Polaris MSA — auto-renew notice window & termination for convenience

Ava / Karthik — thanks for the fast turnaround and for the proposed compromise. Polaris accepts the 60-day opt-out for renewals under the one-year initial term. We also accept the mutual termination framework where the customer chooses transition support or a 30-day fee.

Couple of asks before we send to Legal:
- Please confirm if our Order Form will show any \"Dedicated Capacity\" line items. If not, we will not need the 90-day carve-out.
- Please send the tracked redlines (MSA) + updated Order Form (showing any dedicated SKU if applicable).

We aim to finish Legal review by 7/20 so procurement can complete signatures by 7/23. If you can push the redlines and DocuSign envelope this week that would be ideal.

Thanks,
Elaine Wang
Senior Procurement Manager
Polaris Health
[Attachment: OrderForm_Polaris_Rev1.pdf (pdf)]

preview:

- Preferred signature method (DocuSign vs Adobe Sign)
- List of subcontractors/subprocessors that will see customer data (we need the list for our assessment)

Date: 2026-07-12T10:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: DPA and subprocessors — initial intake

Hi Sophie — thanks for the note.

Quick answers up front: we support DocuSign and Adobe Sign but DocuSign is our usual path; we can generate an envelope. We maintain a current subprocessor list and include it as an annex when executing DPAs. For EU-US transfers we support inserting the standard EU SCCs into the DPA, or we can provide our transfer addendum if that's preferred — open to whatever is simplest for your legal team.

I’ve attached our standard DPA template (Redwood_DPA_Template_20260712.docx) and a short dataflow summary. Can you confirm:
- Whether Novacorp requires a formal whitelist attestation for subprocessors or a published list updated quarterly
- If you need data residency guarantees (e.g., processing restricted to eu-west-1) or just regional controls

Looping in Ben (Privacy Ops) and Lauren (Legal Ops) to confirm internal routing and signing workflow. We aim to meet your July 26 POC timeline.

Next steps I propose:
1) You return your subprocessor list and any redlines to our DPA
2) We confirm transfer mechanism (SCCs vs Redwood addendum)
3) We prepare DocuSign envelope and route for signatures

Best,
Camila Reyes

title: Top-up request: POC load testing (Veridian)
preview:
From: Jamal Khan <jamal@veridian.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 10 Jul 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Top-up request: POC load testing (Veridian)

Hi Ben,

Thanks again for the demo this week — our team is ready to run a scaled load validation against your streaming generation APIs. The goal is to validate tail latency and throughput under concurrent sessions before we sign off on the pilot. We estimate roughly 600k tokens of generation over a 48-hour window with spikes to 120 concurrent sessions.

Hi Sofia —

Thanks again for the demo today — great to meet the ProcureCo team. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we can unblock procurement and a pilot: 

1) Commercial path check: do you intend to purchase via Purchase Order (PO) or will the team use corporate card for initial pilot purchases? Or would you prefer marketplace billing (AWS/GCP marketplace) if available?
2) Supplier onboarding: do you manage vendor records in Coupa or Ariba? If Coupa, we’ll need your supplier ID / tax info and any required vendor questionnaire fields. If Ariba, tell me whether we should expect an Ariba supplier onboarding request.
3) PR (purchase requisition) readiness: I’m attaching a short PR guidance doc that shows the fields most finance teams ask for (line items, cost center, term, contact for invoices). Can you share your internal PR intake form?

Attachments: redwood-pr-guidance.pdf (short checklist)

Proposed next steps: 
- You: confirm PO vs card or marketplace, share PR intake spreadsheet (if available) and preferred supplier system (Coupa/Ariba).
- We: provide a one-page vendor record template and a recommended line-item breakdown you can paste into your PR system.

If easier, we can loop in your procurement contact — happy to do a 15-min call to walk through the fields so they can raise the PR correctly.

Best,
Ben Carter

- Action items for us: send NDA, draft MSA & DPA, a short mutual action plan with milestone dates, and a security evidence bundle (SOC 2 + data retention + model sandbox access).

Subject: Re: Follow-up from demo — data handling, decision criteria, next steps

Ben — thanks for the clear recap. Your stakeholder list is correct. A few clarifications from our side: 

- Economic buyer: Michael Ortega (SVP Product) — final sign-off on budget and timeline.
- Procurement: Nina Kapoor — usual SLA/contract routing; typical procurement review is ~2–3 weeks depending on redlines.
- Legal: Thomas Reed (in-house counsel) — will own MSA and DPA redlines.
- Security: Priya Shah (Head of InfoSec) — will need SOC 2 Type II, data flow diagrams, and an environment we can test against (sandbox).

Decision criteria prioritized: 1) PHI-safe architecture & contractual DPA, 2) latency/availability guarantees, 3) predictable pricing for pilot.

Procurement asks: If you can provide an initial MSA + DPA and a short summary of what you consider non-negotiable commercial terms, that’ll help speed review. Also, we prefer NDA via DocuSign for our records.

Sara Liu

Subject: Internal: Atlas — stakeholder routing & proposed redline sequencing

Team — copying the stakeholder details from Sara above. Proposed internal owners and sequence so we move quickly without stepping on procurement/legal: 

- Initial step: Send NDA via DocuSign (Ben to send). Quick signature from Atlas (1–2 business days typical).
- Parallel: Provide security evidence pack (SOC 2 summary, data flow diagram) and schedule a 60-min tech sandbox session (Rishi to own).
- Commercial: Marissa to own MSA commercial terms (pricing/term); aim to produce a first-pass MSA with highlighted non-negotiables.
- Legal: After Marissa’s commercial highlights, route to Thomas Reed for legal redlines. Ask for 7–10 business day turnaround on initial legal redline; flag complex items that need escalation.
- Procurement: Nina will run procurement checklist after legal redlines; plan for 3–4 business days if no changes.

Routing notes: Thomas has historically pushed back on broad indemnities and vendor data handling clauses — recommend we prepare an alternate DPA clause set and clarify breach/notification windows up front. Also prepare a limited pilot addendum to keep scope/timeboxed while legal completes MSA.

Deliverables I’ll prepare: NDA (DocuSign), draft MSA with commercial callouts, DPA template, and a 3-step mutual action plan spreadsheet. I attached a routing matrix that maps owner -> expected turn time.

Please review owners and the proposed SLA for legal turn-times; if you see potential blockers (e.g., Thomas requires redline windows longer than 10 business days), call it out now so I can reset the plan with the customer.

Thanks,
Ben

- Action items for us: send NDA, draft MSA & DPA, a short mutual action plan with milestone dates, and a security evidence bundle (SOC 2 + data retention + model sandbox access).

- Procurement: Nina Kapoor — usual SLA/contract routing; typical procurement review is ~2–3 weeks depending on redlines.
- Legal: Thomas Reed (in-house counsel) — will own MSA and DPA redlines.
- Security: Priya Shah (Head of InfoSec) — will need SOC 2 Type II, data flow diagrams, and an environment we can test against (sandbox).

- Commercial: Marissa to own MSA commercial terms (pricing/term); aim to produce a first-pass MSA with highlighted non-negotiables.
- Legal: After Marissa’s commercial highlights, route to Thomas Reed for legal redlines. Ask for 7–10 business day turnaround on initial legal redline; flag complex items that need escalation.
- Procurement: Nina will run procurement checklist after legal redlines; plan for 3–4 business days if no changes.

Routing notes: Thomas has historically pushed back on broad indemnities and vendor data handling clauses — recommend we prepare an alternate DPA clause set and clarify breach/notification windows up front. Also prepare a limited pilot addendum to keep scope/timeboxed while legal completes MSA.

Deliverables I’ll prepare: NDA (DocuSign), draft MSA with commercial callouts, DPA template, and a 3-step mutual action plan spreadsheet. I attached a routing matrix that maps owner -> expected turn time.

Please review owners and the proposed SLA for legal turn-times; if you see potential blockers (e.g., Thomas requires redline windows longer than 10 business days), call it out now so I can reset the plan with the customer.

Thanks,
Ben

title: BluePeak case study draft — review & redlines
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marta Ruiz <marta.ruiz@bluepeakmed.com>
Cc: BluePeak Legal <legal@bluepeakmed.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Jul 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: BluePeak case study draft — review & redlines

Hi Marta,

Attached is draft v2 of the BluePeak case study — thank you again for agreeing to be a reference. A few asks from our side:
- Can you fact-check the customer metrics on page 2 (we currently call out a 20% inference cost reduction). Please confirm the exact figure and which baseline was used.
- Legal: please review the data usage / hosting language (we reference \"hosted inference\" in a way that might be sensitive).
- Marketing: confirm logo file and preferred short quote (one or two lines) from your VP of Engineering if possible.
- Timeline: are you comfortable with public publishing the week of July 19 if we get final sign-off by next Friday?

I've pasted the key excerpt below for quick review and attached the Word doc with comments turned on.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0123

Attachments: bluepeak_case_draft_v2.docx


From: Marta Ruiz <marta.ruiz@bluepeakmed.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: BluePeak Legal <legal@bluepeakmed.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Jul 2026 15:03:12 -0700
Subject: Re: BluePeak case study draft — review & redlines

Hi Ben,

Thanks for sending this over — appreciate the clean draft. We reviewed internally and have a few redlines (attached PDF with comments):
- The cost reduction number should read 18% (measured versus our prior CPU-based inference pipeline). Please replace 20% with 18% and update the footnote about the baseline.
- Please change the language that says \"we productionized in two weeks\" — that overstates the timeline. Suggest \"pilot completed in two weeks, ramping to production over subsequent quarters.\"
- We need marketing approval on the quote attribution. Our VP Eng is okay with an abbreviated line, but I need to confirm exact wording.
- Legal flagged the line about \"we host customer data\" — I copied Legal on this thread.

I attached redlines: redlines_bluepeak_20260706.pdf. Will follow up with a final approval after marketing signs off.

Best,
Marta Ruiz
Director, Product Marketing | BluePeak Medical
marta.ruiz@bluepeakmed.com | +1 (617) 222-3344

title: Post-call: NovaHealth perf targets + streaming/throughput checklist
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha@novahealth.com>, Kevin Liu <kevin.liu@novahealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 06 Jul 2026 14:12:00 -0700
Subject: Post-call: NovaHealth perf targets + streaming/throughput checklist

Thanks for the call earlier — quick recap and proposed next steps so we have alignment going into a short POC. High-level asks we captured from your side:

- Primary SLOs: p95 end-to-end < 120 ms; p99 end-to-end < 350 ms (for short RL prompt flows, median payloads ~60 tokens).
- Throughput target: sustained 120 QPS (burst to 300 QPS for 30s) with concurrency per instance up to ~50 concurrent streams.
- Streaming behavior: client expects token-level streaming (first token < 50 ms after model emits) and ability to stream partial generations for long-form flows.
- Failure modes: graceful fallback to shorter responses / lower-cost model when tail latency spikes; observability into token-level timings.

Immediate asks from Redwood so we can scope the POC:
- A sample traffic profile (by request size, tokens in/out, peek at 95/99 quantiles) — this will let us configure batching and KV cache warmup.
- A representative prompt set for the three primary flows (triage, summarization, recommendation).
- Confirmation that NovaHealth can give a 3-day replay window for the POC (or a synthetic generator we can use).

Proposed POC plan (two-week cadence):
1) Day 0–2: ingest traffic profile + replay in staging (no production tokens).
2) Day 3–7: run sustained throughput tests at 1x and 2.5x target; measure p95/p99 and token-level emission latencies.
3) Day 8–10: streaming acceptance (client-side checks for first-token latency and partial chunking).
4) Day 11–14: soak + run failure-mode scenarios (cold start, model fallback).

Attachments: redwood_latency_playbook_v1.pdf (playbook + measurement checklist).

Next steps I propose:
- Kevin: can you share the traffic CSV and a short prompt sample? (see attachment request)
- Samantha: are you ok with the two-week POC window?
- Marissa / Karthik: I'll need pricing/SLA guardrails for a 2-week POC + optional dedicated short burst capacity.

Thanks — I’ll put a calendar invite for POC kickoff once we have the traffic sample.

—
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com



From: Kevin Liu <kevin.liu@novahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Samantha Lee <samantha@novahealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 06 Jul 2026 16:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: NovaHealth perf targets + streaming/throughput checklist

Thanks Ben — attaching the two artifacts we discussed: novahealth_traffic_profile.csv and a small prompt set (in the sheet). Highlights:
- Median request: 58 tokens in / 42 tokens out; tail (95th) ~200 tokens out.
- Typical concurrency per user session is 6–12; internal load balancer sends ~80–150 concurrent requests from the client side during peak.
- For streaming: we expect partial updates at ~200ms cadence for long-form flows; but for the short RL flows we want first token within 75ms ideally.

Question for Redwood: in your hosted mode do you expose per-request token timing traces? We’ll need them to correlate client-side perceived latency vs server-side emission. Also wrapping up compliance review — will need an IP & data handling note for the POC (we can redact PHI for test traffic).

Attachment: novahealth_traffic_profile.csv (CSV, 120KB)

-Kevin
Kevin Liu
Staff Engineer, Infrastructure | NovaHealth
kevin.liu@novahealth.com



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Kevin Liu <kevin.liu@novahealth.com>, Samantha Lee <samantha@novahealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 07 Jul 2026 10:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: NovaHealth perf targets + streaming/throughput checklist

Appreciate the CSV, Kevin — that’s perfect. Quick answers and clarifications on Redwood hosted behavior and what we’ll measure in the POC:

- Per-request traces: yes — our hosted API can emit token-level timing traces (emission timestamps per chunk) and a top-level timeline (reception, queue wait, kernel exec, token emit). We’ll provide a small collector that maps request IDs to those metrics for the POC.
- Cold starts / KV cache: when traffic shifts to a new prompt distribution we typically see a brief warm-up (< 2s) while KV cache fills; our continuous batching approach also smooths tail spikes. We’ll run a cache warmup pass during Day 1 of the POC.
- Streaming cadence: we can tune chunk emission intervals; for short flows we can prioritize single-token microflush to hit first-token latency targets. For long-form the SDK can request chunk sizes or time-based flush (e.g., every 80ms).
- Fallback behavior: we can configure an automatic fallback to a quantized compatible variant when observed p99 exceeds threshold for > 30s; we’ll log each fallback event and include it in the post-POC report.

Action items for POC scope and measurement: we’ll need the traffic replay (CSV) + a mapping of which prompts are critical for p95 (you annotated some in the CSV). We’ll also ask for an opt-in client-side SDK snippet to validate first-byte-timing from the browser side.

I’ve attached a short playbook (redwood_latency_playbook_v1.pdf) that lists required measurements and the exact CSV columns we need.

Planned deliverables after the two-week POC:
- Per-flow p50/p95/p99 for end-to-end latency (client to client)
- Token emission timelines (first-token, inter-token)
- Recommendations: batch sizing, KV warmup schedule, and quantization profile if cost/latency trade-off suggests it

Attachment: redwood_latency_playbook_v1.pdf

—
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com



From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Samantha Lee <samantha@novahealth.com>, Kevin Liu <kevin.liu@novahealth.com>
Date: Wed, 08 Jul 2026 08:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: NovaHealth perf targets + streaming/throughput checklist

Looping in Karthik for pricing / SLA notes. Quick bullets for internal reference:
- POC pricing: usage-based credits + short reserved burst pool for the 2-week window (estimate: $6k–$9k depending on 2.5x burst usage).
- SLA ask: for demo/POC we propose best-effort (no formal SLA), but we’ll surface latency baselines and an operational runbook.
- If NovaHealth wants SLA-backed Dedicated we can scope a separate contract after acceptance.

Karthik — any additional guardrails we should propose for the PSC?

—Marissa
Marissa Cole
Director, Solutions Strategy | Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwood.com



From: Samantha Lee <samantha@novahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Cc: Kevin Liu <kevin.liu@novahealth.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace.oconnor@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 09 Jul 2026 09:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-call: NovaHealth perf targets + streaming/throughput checklist

Thanks team — this looks doable and we’re happy with the proposed two-week plan. Quick confirmations:

- We’ll provide the sanitized CSV and prompt mapping by EOD Monday.
- We accept the POC pricing estimate for now; we need an internal purchase order but can proceed once the CSV is in.
- We’d like the POC kickoff on Tuesday morning (09:00 PT) if that works for Redwood.

Also can you send a short note about data handling for the POC (what logs are retained, retention period, and redaction guidance)? Kevin will complete the compliance checklist once we have that.

If that all works, I’ll sign the short POC engagement form and we can go.

Best,
Samantha Lee
Head of Product | NovaHealth
samantha@novahealth.com

Subject: Weekly MAP gatekeeper update — POC acceptance criteria & blockers

Hi Ethan & Claire,

Quick weekly MAP update from our side for the Nimbus POC. Keeping this short with explicit owners so we can close by the end of July per the timeline in the MAP. Highlights and ask at the top:

- Ask: Confirm the acceptance window for the Clinical Search test (see acceptance item 2) by EOD Friday so we can schedule load tests.
- Net timeline: still aiming for pilot acceptance by 2026-07-29; risk if procurement delays or if tokenization edge cases require a hotfix.

- Eval plan: on track. We uploaded NI-PoC-Eval-Checklist.pdf to the shared drive (link below). Vanessa has completed the model baseline runs for the demo dataset.
- Acceptance criteria: final draft attached in Nimbus_MAP_v3.xlsx — items 1–5 are stable, item 6 (audit log retention) waiting on procurement decision.
- Blockers: procurement needs legal sign-off for data retention clause; a reproducible search relevance discrepancy seen on a 0.5% subset (Vanessa notes).

Meeting notes (from last sync) — action items follow
- MAP owner: Ben Carter — cadence weekly until acceptance.
- SE lead: Vanessa Ortiz — responsible for test harness and reproduction steps for relevance discrepancy.
- CS lead: Marissa Cole — will coordinate acceptance QA with Claire.
- Procurement touchpoint: Lauren Bishop — to confirm redline timeline with Nimbus procurement.

Action items (owner — due)
1) Procurement redline status update — Lauren — 2026-07-03
2) Confirm Clinical Search acceptance window (3-day window) — Ethan/Claire — 2026-07-03 (EOD)
3) Repro steps + minimized failing set (for relevance discrepancy) — Vanessa — 2026-07-05
4) Run end-to-end acceptance harness and upload results — Vanessa/Marissa — 2026-07-20
5) Run security checklist for private endpoint (KMS proof) — Lauren/Marissa — 2026-07-10

Risks & mitigation
- If procurement redline takes >7 business days, propose a partial acceptance (functional signoff) to avoid slipping the close.
- If the relevance discrepancy persists after the minimized failing set is isolated, we will schedule a deep-dive with applied ML (Marissa to triage).

Next touch: weekly MAP checkpoint meeting — 2026-07-08 @ 10:00 PT (invite sent). Please reply with any schedule conflicts.

- Completions: ~420M tokens/month

- Ballpark monthly budget: $40k–$60k for baseline; willing to discuss commit/discounting for stability.

3) Are there peak-month forecasts (dates) we can use to size burst capacity?

Attachments included: sample_prompts.jsonl (10 representative prompts), context_profile.csv (token-length distribution).

- Schedule 60-min technical deep-dive next week to walk Marcus through latency/batching tradeoffs (can you do Tue 7/7 or Wed 7/8?)
- You to confirm whether compliance questionnaire will be sent; we can loop in security if needed.

Thanks — I’ll follow with the draft pricing sheet and a short note from Marcus on unit economics.

Best,
